title: LLM Serving — Production Inference at Scale
description: Serving LLMs in production requires orchestrating GPU memory, managing latency SLOs, and maximizing throughput under cost constraints. FAANG system design interviews frequently ask you to design an LLM serving platform from scratch.

sections.title: The Serving Stack: GPU → Scheduler → Load Balancer
sections.description:
A production LLM serving system has four layers:

1. GPU Layer: Nvidia A100 (80GB) or H100 (80GB) GPUs. Models are sharded across GPUs using tensor/pipeline parallelism. Each GPU group is an inference pod.

2. Inference Engine: The runtime that runs the model forward pass. Options: vLLM (best throughput, PagedAttention), TensorRT-LLM (Nvidia, lowest latency), TGI (HuggingFace, easiest), SGLang (structured generation).

3. Scheduler / Request Router: Manages request queue, batching, and dispatching. Strategies: FCFS (fair), shortest-job-first (prioritize short prompts), SLA-aware (priority lanes).

4. Load Balancer: Distributes requests across inference pods. Keys: consistent hashing for prefix cache locality, request affinity for KV cache reuse, circuit breakers for failed pods.

FAANG interview tip: Always propose a 4-layer architecture — it shows you've thought about serving beyond just the model.
sections.codeBlock:
# Pseudo-architecture of LLM serving system
class LLMServingPlatform:
    def __init__(self, pods, engine_type='vllm'):
        self.pods = pods                 # list of (gpu_group, model_shard)
        self.scheduler = RequestScheduler()
        self.lb = LoadBalancer(pods)
        self.engine = engine_type

    async def serve(self, request):
        # 1. Route to pod (consistent hash on user_id + prompt_prefix)
        pod = self.lb.route(request)
        # 2. Enqueue in scheduler (supports priority, preemption)
        result = await self.scheduler.schedule(pod, request)
        # 3. Wait for generation to complete
        return result

class LoadBalancer:
    def route(self, request):
        # Consistent hashing ensures same prefix hits same pod (KV cache hit)
        key = hash(request.user_id)  # or hash(request.prompt[:50])
        return self.pods[key % len(self.pods)]

class RequestScheduler:
    def __init__(self):
        self.queue = asyncio.PriorityQueue()
        self.running = {}  # pod_id -> list of active requests

    async def schedule(self, pod, request):
        # Continuous batching: add to running batch if capacity exists
        if self._can_fit(pod, request):
            token = await pod.infer(request)
            return token
        # Otherwise enqueue with priority
        await self.queue.put((request.priority, request))
        return await self._dispatch_next()

    def _can_fit(self, pod, request):
        # Check if adding this request exceeds KV cache budget
        # ponytail: real impl checks free KV blocks in vLLM
        return pod.free_kv_blocks >= request.estimated_blocks

# ponytail: production systems use mTLS, auth, and canary deployments

sections.title: Continuous Batching: The Core Throughput Optimization
sections.description:
Without batching, each request occupies a full GPU for its entire generate loop — grossly inefficient because decoding is memory-bandwidth bound. Continuous (dynamic) batching interleaves multiple requests at the token level — at each step, the engine processes the next token for every active request in the batch.

How it works:
1. A batch of N requests runs through pre-fill (parallel prompt processing).
2. After pre-fill, each request enters the decode loop — but they proceed together, step by step.
3. When request A finishes (generates EOS), it exits the batch. New request B joins on the next step.
4. The engine runs one forward pass per step, processing all active requests simultaneously.

Throughput impact: Batch size B = 8 → ~6× throughput vs no batching (Amdahl's law — some overhead from attention masking). Batch size B = 64 → ~30× throughput.

Caveat: Latency increases with batch size. Each request's time-to-first-token (TTFT) increases linearly with the total tokens in the batch. The scheduler tunes batch size dynamically to meet latency SLOs.
sections.codeBlock:
# Continuous batching simulator
class ContinuousBatch:
    def __init__(self, engine, max_batch=16):
        self.engine = engine
        self.batch = []   # list of (request_id, tokens, kv_cache, finished)
        self.max_batch = max_batch

    async def submit(self, request):
        if len(self.batch) >= self.max_batch:
            # Wait for a slot
            return await self._wait_and_enqueue(request)
        
        # Pre-fill: process all prompt tokens in parallel
        tokens = await self.engine.prefill(request.prompt_tokens)
        entry = (request.id, tokens, None, False)
        self.batch.append(entry)

    async def step(self):
        """Run one decode step for all active requests."""
        if not self.batch:
            return
        
        # Single forward pass over the combined batch
        # Each request contributes its latest token
        latest_tokens = torch.tensor([e[1][-1] for e in self.batch])  # [B]
        next_logits = await self.engine.forward(latest_tokens, self.batch)
        
        # Sample next token for each request
        new_batch = []
        finished_ids = []
        for i, (req_id, tokens, kv, _) in enumerate(self.batch):
            next_id = sample(next_logits[i])
            tokens = tokens + [next_id]
            if next_id == EOS_TOKEN_ID:
                finished_ids.append(req_id)
                # Emit complete result
            else:
                new_batch.append((req_id, tokens, kv, False))
        
        self.batch = new_batch
        return finished_ids

    # ponytail: real vLLM uses CUDA graphs, flash attention, and
    # separates pre-fill and decode into distinct scheduling phases

sections.title: Model Parallelism: Tensor & Pipeline
sections.description:
When a model doesn't fit on one GPU, you must shard it across multiple GPUs. Two main strategies — and a FAANG system design interview will expect you to compare them:

Tensor Parallelism (TP): Split each layer's weights across GPUs. For a linear layer W of shape [d_out, d_in], GPU 0 holds W[:, :d_in/2], GPU 1 holds W[:, d_in/2:]. Each GPU computes its portion, then all-reduce to combine. Requires high-bandwidth interconnects (NVLink: 600GB/s on H100). Scaling efficiency ~80% at TP=8.

Pipeline Parallelism (PP): Assign contiguous layers to different GPUs. GPU 0 runs layers 1-10, GPU 1 runs layers 11-20, etc. Micro-batch pipelining keeps all GPUs busy. Does not require high inter-GPU bandwidth (only sends activations, not weights). Scaling efficiency ~70% at PP=8 due to pipeline bubbles.

FAANG recommendation: TP=8 within a node (NVLink), PP=2 across nodes (slower interconnect). This hybrid 'TP+PP' is used by vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, and virtually all production deployments of 70B+ models.
sections.codeBlock:
# Tensor Parallelism — splitting a Linear layer across 2 GPUs
# GPU 0 code:
class TPLinear(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_features, out_features, rank, world_size):
        super().__init__()
        assert world_size == 2  # simplified: TP=2
        self.rank = rank
        # Split out_features: GPU0 gets first half, GPU1 gets second half
        local_out = out_features // world_size
        self.weight = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(local_out, in_features))
        
    def forward(self, x):
        # Local computation (each GPU does its share)
        local_out = x @ self.weight.T  # [B, T, local_out]
        # All-reduce to combine both GPUs' outputs
        # torch.distributed.all_reduce(local_out)
        return local_out

# Pipeline Parallelism — Layer groups with micro-batches
# global_rank=0 holds layers 0-5, global_rank=1 holds layers 6-11
# With micro-batches = 4:
#   GPU0: process mb1_l1→l5, then mb2_l1→l5, then mb3, then mb4
#   GPU1: wait for mb1, process mb1_l6→l11, then mb2, then mb3, then mb4
# Bubble overhead = (PP - 1) / (PP * micro_batches) = ~9% for PP=2, m=4

def pipeline_schedule(n_layers, pp_size, micro_batches):
    layers_per_gpu = n_layers // pp_size
    # Bubble ratio: fraction of time GPUs are idle
    bubble = (pp_size - 1) / (pp_size * micro_batches)
    return layers_per_gpu, bubble

print(pipeline_schedule(32, 4, 8))  # 8 layers/GPU, ~9.4% bubble
print(pipeline_schedule(32, 8, 4))  # 4 layers/GPU, ~21.9% bubble

# ponytail: 1F1B scheduling reduces bubble vs pure forward pass

sections.title: Latency SLOs: TTFT, TPOT & Throughput Modeling
sections.description:
LLM serving has three key metrics that system design interviews test:

TTFT (Time to First Token): Time from request arrival to first output token. Determined by pre-fill phase. Target: < 200ms for interactive chat, < 1s for streaming. Scales linearly with prompt length. Optimized by: FlashAttention, prompt caching, KV cache prefix sharing.

TPOT (Time Per Output Token): Time for each subsequent token. Determined by decode phase. Target: < 25ms/token (40 tok/s) for interactive chat. Memory-bandwidth bound — bottleneck is reading KV cache from VRAM, not compute. Optimized by: speculative decoding, INT4 KV cache quantization, larger batch size (amortize weight read across more tokens).

Throughput (tokens/second): Total system output. Target: maximize tokens generated per second across all requests. Tradeoff with latency: larger batches increase throughput but increase TPOT.

Pareto frontier: For a given GPU budget, there's a tradeoff surface between latency (TTFT + TPOT) and throughput. The optimal operating point depends on the application: chat applications need low latency, batch processing (offline summarization) can optimize for throughput.
sections.codeBlock:
# Serving capacity planning

def estimate_gpu_capacity(
    n_gpus,
    model_params,
    avg_prompt_len=512,
    avg_gen_len=256,
    kv_cache_per_seq=1.07e9,  # Llama 3 8B @ 8K context (from kv-cache guide)
    gpu_memory=80e9,          # A100 80GB
    overhead_factor=0.85       # ~15% reserved for OS, CUDA context
):
    """
    Estimate concurrent requests a given GPU fleet can handle.
    """
    weights_memory = model_params * 2  # FP16
    available = n_gpus * gpu_memory * overhead_factor
    non_kv_memory = weights_memory      # weights in VRAM
    kv_memory_budget = available - non_kv_memory
    
    max_concurrent = kv_memory_budget / kv_cache_per_seq
    
    # Throughput estimate (simplified)
    # At batch=B, each step processes B tokens in ~25ms (memory-bound)
    tokens_per_step = max_concurrent
    time_per_step = 0.025  # seconds
    throughput = tokens_per_step / time_per_step
    
    return {
        'max_concurrent': int(max_concurrent),
        'kv_memory_gb': kv_memory_budget / 1e9,
        'estimated_throughput_tok/s': throughput
    }

# Example: 8× A100 80GB for Llama 3 70B FP16
specs = estimate_gpu_capacity(
    n_gpus=8, model_params=70e9, 
    kv_cache_per_seq=1.07e9  # assumes 8K context
)
print(f"Max concurrent: {specs['max_concurrent']}")
print(f"Estimated throughput: {specs['estimated_throughput_tok/s']:.0f} tok/s")

# ponytail: real capacity depends on batch scheduling efficiency

sections.title: Prefix Caching & KV Cache Reuse
sections.description:
When multiple requests share a common prefix (e.g., system prompt, few-shot examples, retrieved context), their KV cache for those shared tokens is identical. Prefix caching computes the KV cache for the shared prefix once and reuses it across all requests sharing that prefix.

How it works: The scheduler maintains a radix tree (prefix tree) of KV cache entries, keyed by token sequence. When a new request arrives:
1. Traverse the radix tree with the request's prompt tokens.
2. For the longest matching prefix, reuse cached KV blocks.
3. Pre-fill only the new (non-cached) suffix.

Benefits: 40-60% reduction in pre-fill time for requests sharing a system prompt. 2-5× throughput improvement in multi-tenant settings (e.g., 100 users, same system prompt, different user queries).

Implementation: vLLM supports prefix caching natively via its block manager (set --enable-prefix-caching). SGLang has a similar feature called 'cache-aware scheduling'.

Tradeoff: Maintaining the radix tree has memory overhead. Cache eviction (LRU) is needed to prevent unbounded memory growth. Long-lived caches need to handle 'cache drift' — if the system prompt changes, old cache entries are invalidated.

details:
Speculative Decoding in Production: A small draft model generates K candidate tokens in parallel (cheap). The large target model verifies all K in one forward pass (parallel verification is as fast as generating 1 token). Accepted tokens cost ~1 target model forward pass. Speedup: 2-3× with no quality loss. Used by Google (Medusa), Meta (self-speculative), and Anthropic. Requires careful draft model training — too conservative (few accepted) or too aggressive (many rejected) both lose speedup.

GPU Selection Guide: A100 40GB vs 80GB vs H100 80GB vs H200 141GB. A100 40GB: cheapest, good for <20B models. A100 80GB: workhorse for most deployments (7B-70B). H100 80GB: 2-3× faster than A100 due to FP8 Tensor Cores and faster HBM3. H200 141GB: best for very large models (70B+ with large contexts) — KV cache capacity is the bottleneck.

FAANG interview tip: When asked 'Design YouTube's AI assistant' or 'Design an LLM-powered customer support system' — follow this framework: (1) Model selection (size, MoE vs dense), (2) GPU fleet sizing with KV cache math, (3) Continuous batching scheduler with latency SLOs, (4) Prefix caching for shared system prompts, (5) Speculative decoding for latency reduction, (6) Load balancing with consistent hashing for cache locality, (7) Cost optimization (on-demand vs spot instances, auto-scaling).
